content.text:
Happy to — here's the honest pitch for each, costs included, tailored to *this* project (a non-Cocoa SDL/C game, already shipping signed+notarized DMGs, cut by a time-poor solo maintainer every few days).

## 1. Notify + open page

**The pitch:** On launch, a background HTTPS call to GitHub's API reads the latest release tag and compares it to the version baked into the app. If there's a newer one, a small non-blocking prompt appears — *"Oni 1.3.0r4 is available"* — with **Download** (opens the Releases page) and **Skip / Later**. That's it.

**Why it fits you:** You already have a Cocoa bridge (the first-launch `GameDataFolder` picker is a native dialog), so this is `NSURLSession` + `NSAlert` — a few dozen lines, no new frameworks, no keys. **Your release pipeline doesn't change at all** — `gh release create` already produces exactly what this reads (a tag + a DMG).
**Recurring cost per release: zero.**
**The catch:** it's a *nudge*, not an update. The user still drags the new DMG over. If they dismiss it, they stay put.
**Best for:** shipping this week; niche audience; keeping your time free. The 80/20.

## 2. Full Sparkle 2

**The pitch:** The real macOS answer — the same framework the Feral Oni port shipped. The app downloads, verifies, installs, and **relaunches the new version itself, one click, no Finder**. Optionally fully silent in the background.

**What it costs to build:** embed Sparkle 2 (ARM64-native), sign+notarize its helper apps (your inside-out signing walk already does every dylib, so it slots in), generate an EdDSA key pair (public key in Info.plist, private key guarded in Keychain), and a bit of Obj-C glue to drive it.
**Recurring cost per release:** one scripted step — zip the `.app`, run Sparkle's `generate_appcast` (signs + writes the `appcast.xml`), upload both to the release. Scriptable, but it's a new moving part: a bad appcast or wrong signature breaks **every** user's updater at once. *That's the operational weight the 2026-05-24 design flinched at.*
**Wrinkles for us:** non-Cocoa app means we drive Sparkle programmatically (clean at launch/menu, awkward if a dialog fires mid-fullscreen — so we'd only auto-check at startup); we'd add a zipped-`.app` artifact next to the human DMG; **lose the EdDSA key and you can never push another auto-update to existing installs** — back it up.
**Best for:** when cadence + audience grow. And honestly, your current pace (r1→r2→r3 in three days) is exactly when "users stuck on an old build" hurts most — which argues *for* this eventually.

## 3. Notify + auto-download DMG (middle)

**The pitch:** Same launch check as #1, but the app downloads the DMG itself, mounts it, and reveals it so the user just drags-to-Applications — saving the browser hop.
**Cost:** zero recurring, but it's more code than #1 for a marginal gain. You're reimplementing a sliver of Sparkle (download + progress + **verify** — you'd have to `spctl`-check the DMG before mounting, or you've built a malware vector) without Sparkle's hardening. And a running `.app` still can't replace itself, so the manual drag remains anyway.
**My honest read:** the awkward middle — costs more than #1 for less than #2. I'd only pick it if the browser hop specifically annoys you.

## My take

- **#1 is the clear v1**: ships now, zero recurring cost, kills the "too much burden" objection that parked this, and is forward-compatible — adding Sparkle later doesn't throw any of it away.
- **#2 is the right *destination*** if releases keep flowing and the audience grows.
- **#3 I'd mostly talk you out of.**

So the strongest play is probably **#1 now, graduate to #2 later** — which also respects the prior design's instinct without slamming the door.

One cross-cutting flag regardless of choice: your version strings (`a1`, `r1`, `r3`) aren't directly comparable for "is B newer than A" math. I'd make `CFBundleVersion` a **monotonic integer** used purely for the newer-than check, keeping `CFBundleShortVersionString` as the pretty label. Cheap, and every option needs it.

Good — **the repo is public** (the unauthenticated API returns `200` and lists the releases). So strangers genuinely can download off GitHub, and the app can read the public Releases API with no auth. (Side note: CLAUDE.md and MEMORY.md still call the repo "private" — that's stale; I'll offer to fix it at the end.)

And the startup hook is clear: [Oni.c:193-199](OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c:193) runs the data picker pre-window on the main thread; the update check slots in right after data is resolved, same model.

I have everything I need. Here's the design.

---

## Design: GitHub release-update notifier (v1)

### 1. What the user sees
On launch, if a newer release exists, a native dialog appears before the game window (same moment as the data picker):

> **Oni 1.3.0r4 is available**
> You're running 1.3.0r3. Version 1.3.0r4 is now available on GitHub.
> [ Download… ]  [ Skip This Version ]  [ Later ]
> ☐ Don't check for updates

- **Download…** → opens the release page in the browser (notes + DMG), then the game continues launching normally.
- **Skip This Version** → never prompt for *that* tag again.
- **Later** → no-op; may prompt again next eligible launch.
- **Suppression checkbox** → disables checks entirely (privacy/opt-out).

If offline, rate-limited, or already current → nothing happens, launch proceeds. Worst case it adds a short bounded wait (~2 s cap) to launch.

### 2. Architecture (mirrors your existing idiom)
Same split as the data picker — **pure C core + thin Cocoa shell**:

- **`ONi_UpdateCheck.c` / `.h`** (new, pure C, unit-testable): the decision function. Inputs `(my_version, my_build_time, latest_tag, latest_published_at, skipped_tag)` → `{none | update_available}` plus the display name + URL to surface. No Cocoa, no network. Mirrors `ONi_GameData.c`.
- **`Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm` / `.h`** (new, Cocoa shell): throttle → `NSURLSession` fetch → `NSJSONSerialization` parse → call the C decision fn → `NSAlert` → `NSWorkspace openURL:` → persist state. Exposes one C entry point `void ONrUpdateCheck_RunAtStartup(void)`.
- **Call site**: `Oni.c`, right after game-data resolution (~line 200), under the existing `#if defined(__APPLE__) && UUmSDL` guard — one include + one call.
- **Build wiring**: add the two new files to the macOS `.mm` block in [OniProj/OniCMakeProjs/OniProj/CMakeLists.txt:556](OniARM64/OniProj/OniCMakeProjs/OniProj/CMakeLists.txt) (AppKit/Foundation already linked there).

### 3. The "is it newer?" logic
- **Source**: `GET https://api.github.com/repos/andiyar/OniARM64/releases?per_page=10` (unauthenticated; public repo). UA header + 5 s timeout.
- **Pick latest**: among results with `draft == false`, the max `published_at`. **Prereleases included** (your channel choice: newest build wins).
- **Compare**: prompt **iff** `latest.tag ≠ my version` **and** `latest.published_at > my build time` **and** `latest.tag ≠ skipped tag`.
  - My version = `CFBundleShortVersionString` from Info.plist (normalise the tag's leading `v`).
  - The **build-time guard** baked in at compile time (CMake) exists only to stop *local dev builds* from nagging us; for end users on a released DMG, tag-difference is the real signal. This deliberately **sidesteps fragile `a1`/`r1`/`r3` version parsing** — no semver comparator to get wrong.

### 4. State & throttling (`NSUserDefaults`)
- `OniUpdateSkippedTag` (string) · `OniUpdateLastCheck` (date) · `OniUpdateDisabled` (bool).
- Throttle: skip the network call if checked < ~4 h ago, so relaunches stay instant.
- Privacy: only outbound call is to `api.github.com`; suppression checkbox turns it off for good.

### 5. Per-release impact: **zero**
You keep cutting releases exactly as today (`gh release create vX…`). The app reads tags live — no new file, no signing, no appcast, no key. The only soft ask: give releases a tidy `name`, since the prompt may show it.

### 6. What this sets up for Sparkle later
The decision logic and the "surface a prompt here" seam are reusable. Graduating to Sparkle = embed the framework + publish an appcast (same "latest manifest" idea, plus signatures and in-app install). Optional cheap prep now: make `CFBundleVersion` a **monotonic integer** (Sparkle compares it) — but v1's timestamp logic doesn't need it.

### 7. Risks / edges
- **GitHub rate limit** (60/hr/IP unauth): once-per-launch + 4 h throttle → negligible; if hit, treated as "no info," skip.
- **AppKit readiness**: when the data picker *didn't* run (returning users), `NSApplication` isn't finish-launched yet; the shell ensures AppKit is ready idempotently before showing the alert (the picker already shows the pattern).
- **Network slowness**: hard ~2 s cap on the pre-window wait; never blocks launch beyond that.
- **CLAUDE.md staleness**: "private repo" references are wrong now — offer to fix.

### 8. Out of scope (deferred)
In-engine overlay (vs native dialog) · auto-download/auto-install (that's Sparkle) · channel picker · "Check for Updates" menu item · CI-built releases.

---

Does this look right? In particular: (a) **native dialog at launch** vs waiting until the in-game menu, and (b) the **timestamp-based compare** instead of parsing version strings — those are the two calls I'd most want your eyes on before I write it up as a spec.
Approved. Let me lock it into a spec. Per the project's workflow contract I'll open a tracking issue first (so the spec/commit can reference it), then write and commit the design doc.

## Problem

input.content:
# OniARM64 — GitHub release-update notifier (v1)

**Date**: 2026-06-05
**Status**: Approved design, ready for implementation plan
**Closes**: [issue #40](https://github.com/andiyar/OniARM64/issues/40) (update notifier — notify-only stage)
**Relates**: [issue #13](https://github.com/andiyar/OniARM64/issues/13) (Developer ID signing — the Sparkle v2 path this defers to)

## Goal

When someone runs `OniARM64.app` downloaded from the public [Releases page](https://github.com/andiyar/OniARM64/releases), the app should tell them when a newer release exists — instead of silently leaving them stranded on a stale build. After this lands, launching an out-of-date app pops a native dialog ("Oni 1.3.0r4 is available — Download… / Skip This Version / Later"), Download opens the release page in the browser, and the user installs the new DMG exactly as they do today.

This is the **notify-only** stage. True one-click in-app self-update (Sparkle) is deliberately a later, separate stage — see *Forward-compatibility* and the *Out of scope* sections. The notify stage was chosen because it carries **zero recurring per-release burden**, which is the exact reason Sparkle was parked in the [2026-05-24 Developer ID signing design](2026-05-24-developer-id-signing-design.md).

## Decisions made during brainstorming

| Decision | Choice |
| --- | --- |
| Stage | Notify-only now; Sparkle later (separate issue). v1 is forward-compatible with it |
| What "auto-update" does | Detect a newer release and prompt; user downloads + installs the DMG manually (no in-app install) |
| Channel | **Newest build wins, prereleases included.** Right now every release is a prerelease, and everyone running the bundle is a preview tester who wants the latest |
| Data source | Unauthenticated `GET /repos/andiyar/OniARM64/releases` (repo is **public** — verified 2026-06-05, unauth API returns `200`) |
| Version comparison | **Timestamp-based**, not version-string parsing. Prompt iff `latest.tag ≠ mine` AND `latest.published_at > my build time` AND `latest.tag ≠ skipped tag`. Sidesteps fragile `a1`/`r1`/`r3` parsing |
| Build-time guard | A compile-time epoch baked via CMake, used only to stop local dev builds self-nagging; for end users on a released DMG, tag-difference is the real signal |
| Where the prompt lives | Native `NSAlert` at startup (pre-window, main thread) — same model as the existing data picker. NOT an in-engine overlay |
| Architecture | Pure C decision core (`ONi_UpdateCheck.c`, unit-tested) + thin Cocoa shell (`Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm`). Mirrors `ONi_GameData.c` / `Oni_DataSetup_macOS.mm` |
| Network in C vs Obj-C | Fetch + JSON parse + ISO8601→epoch all in the `.mm` (NSURLSession / NSJSONSerialization / NSISO8601DateFormatter). The C core does pure string/int comparison only — no date libs, no I/O |
| State | `NSUserDefaults`: skipped tag, last-check timestamp, opt-out flag |
| Throttle | Skip the network call if checked < ~4 h ago, so relaunches stay instant |
| Launch safety | Hard ~2.5 s cap on the pre-window network wait; offline/slow/rate-limited → silently skip, launch proceeds |
| Opt-out | `NSAlert` suppression checkbox ("Don't check for updates") sets a persistent flag |
| Per-release burden | **Zero.** `gh release create` flow unchanged; no keys, no appcast, no embedded updater |

## Architecture

```
Oni.c (startup, main thread, after game-data resolution, pre-SDL-window)
  └─ ONrUpdateCheck_RunAtStartup()                 [Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm]
        ├─ read NSUserDefaults (disabled? throttled?)         → maybe return early
        ├─ ensure AppKit ready (idempotent; mirrors picker)
        ├─ NSURLSession GET /releases  (UA header, ~2.5s cap) → array of releases
        ├─ parse JSON → ONtReleaseInfo[]  (published_at via NSISO8601DateFormatter)
        ├─ ONrUpdateCheck_PickLatest(...)                     [ONi_UpdateCheck.c, pure]
        ├─ ONrUpdateCheck_Decide(...)                         [ONi_UpdateCheck.c, pure]
        └─ if Available: NSAlert (Download… / Skip / Later + suppression)
              ├─ Download → NSWorkspace openURL: release html_url
              ├─ Skip     → persist skipped tag
              └─ (always)  → persist last-check timestamp
```

Same split the codebase already uses for first-run setup: all the decidable logic is pure, testable C (`ONi_UpdateCheck.c`); the `.mm` is the thin Cocoa shell doing network + dialog. The check runs at the same pre-window, main-thread point as `ONrDataSetup_RunGuidedPicker` ([Oni.c:193](../../../OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c)), so there is no interaction with SDL's event loop or fullscreen state.

## Components

### 1. `ONi_UpdateCheck.c` / `ONi_UpdateCheck.h` — new (pure C, unit-tested)

No Cocoa, no network, no date parsing. Trivially unit-testable.

```c
// ONi_UpdateCheck.h
#define ONcUpdate_TagMax    64
#define ONcUpdate_NameMax   160
#define ONcUpdate_UrlMax    512

typedef enum {
    ONcUpdate_None      = 0,   // up to date, can't tell, or skipped
    ONcUpdate_Available = 1    // a newer, non-skipped release exists
} ONtUpdateVerdict;

typedef struct {
    char     tag[ONcUpdate_TagMax];     // e.g. "v1.3.0r4"
    char     name[ONcUpdate_NameMax];   // display, e.g. "Oni 1.3.0r4 — …" (falls back to tag)
    char     url[ONcUpdate_UrlMax];     // release html_url to open
    long long published_at;             // epoch seconds (filled by the .mm)
    int      is_draft;                  // 1 = draft (always excluded)
    int      is_prerelease;             // recorded; v1 includes prereleases
} ONtReleaseInfo;

// Pick the newest non-draft release. Returns index, or -1 if none.
// Channel policy lives here: v1 ignores is_prerelease (includes all);
// a future "stable only" filter is a one-line change + one test.
int ONrUpdateCheck_PickLatest(const ONtReleaseInfo *releases, int count);

// Pure decision. my_build_time == 0 disables the dev-build guard.
ONtUpdateVerdict ONrUpdateCheck_Decide(
    const char           *my_version,    // CFBundleShortVersionString, e.g. "1.3.0r3"
    long long             my_build_time, // compile-time epoch, 0 = unknown
    const ONtReleaseInfo *latest,        // result of PickLatest, or NULL
    const char           *skipped_tag);  // "" if none
```

**Decision rule** (`ONrUpdateCheck_Decide`), in order:
1. `latest == NULL` → `None`.
2. `latest->tag` equals `skipped_tag` (exact) → `None`.
3. normalise both tags by stripping a single leading `v`; if `latest` normalised == `my_version` → `None` (already on it).
4. `my_build_time != 0` && `latest->published_at <= my_build_time` → `None` (we're newer/equal — local dev-build guard).
5. otherwise → `Available`.

### 2. `Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm` / `Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h` — new (Cocoa shell)

Exposes one C entry point:

```c
// Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h
void ONrUpdateCheck_RunAtStartup(void);   // no-op on non-Apple builds
```

Flow:
1. **Gate**: if `OniUpdateDisabled` is set → return. If `OniUpdateLastCheck` is within the throttle window (~4 h) → return.
2. **AppKit readiness**: if `NSApp` is not yet running (returning users, where the data picker did not run), set `NSApplicationActivationPolicyRegular` + `finishLaunching` + activate — idempotent, mirroring `ONrDataSetup_RunGuidedPicker`. Guard against a double `finishLaunching`.
3. **Fetch**: `NSURLSession` data task to `https://api.github.com/repos/andiyar/OniARM64/releases?per_page=10`, headers `Accept: application/vnd.github+json` and `User-Agent: OniARM64-UpdateCheck` (GitHub requires a UA). Bounded wait ~2.5 s via a dispatch semaphore; on expiry, cancel and return **without** writing `lastCheck` (so a transient offline launch retries next time).
4. **Parse**: `NSJSONSerialization` → fill `ONtReleaseInfo[]`; `published_at` parsed with `NSISO8601DateFormatter` → epoch seconds.
5. **Decide**: `ONrUpdateCheck_PickLatest` then `ONrUpdateCheck_Decide`, passing `my_version` from `[[NSBundle mainBundle] objectForInfoDictionaryKey:@"CFBundleShortVersionString"]` and `my_build_time` from the `ONI_BUILD_EPOCH` compile-time macro.
6. **Prompt** (only if `Available`): `NSAlert`, `messageText` = release name, `informativeText` = "You're running <mine>. Version <new> is now available on GitHub.", buttons **Download…** / **Skip This Version** / **Later**, plus `showsSuppressionButton = YES` ("Don't check for updates"). On Download → `[[NSWorkspace sharedWorkspace] openURL:]` the release `html_url`. On Skip → persist `OniUpdateSkippedTag`. If suppression checked → persist `OniUpdateDisabled`.
7. **Always on a successful fetch**: persist `OniUpdateLastCheck = now`.

`Download` opens the page and returns; the game continues launching (non-disruptive — the user can update after playing).

### 3. `Oni.c` — call site (modified)

- Add `#include "Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h"` near the existing macOS includes (~[Oni.c:66](../../../OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c)).
- Immediately after game-data resolution + its error check (~[Oni.c:202](../../../OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c), before `TMrInitialize`):

```c
#if defined(__APPLE__) && UUmSDL
	ONrUpdateCheck_RunAtStartup();
#endif
```

Data is guaranteed resolved at this point, and we're still pre-window on the main thread.

### 4. `CMakeLists.txt` — modified

In the macOS native Obj-C++ block of [OniProj/OniCMakeProjs/OniProj/CMakeLists.txt:556](../../../OniARM64/OniProj/OniCMakeProjs/OniProj/CMakeLists.txt) (where `Oni_DataSetup_macOS.mm` is added), add:

- Sources: `ONi_UpdateCheck.c`, `ONi_UpdateCheck.h`, `Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm`, `Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h`.
- Compile definition for the build-time guard:

```cmake
string(TIMESTAMP ONI_BUILD_EPOCH "%s" UTC)   # seconds since epoch, at configure time
target_compile_definitions(<target> PRIVATE ONI_BUILD_EPOCH=${ONI_BUILD_EPOCH})
```

`ONi_UpdateCheck.c` compiles on all platforms (pure C); the `.mm` and the `Oni.c` call are already `__APPLE__`/`UUmSDL`-guarded. AppKit/Foundation are already linked for the existing `.mm` units. If `ONI_BUILD_EPOCH` is ever undefined, the code defaults `my_build_time` to `0`, which disables only the dev-build guard (tag-difference still works).

### 5. Unit tests — new

Mirror the existing `ONi_GameData` C-core tests (same harness/location — the plan pins the exact path). Cover `ONrUpdateCheck_PickLatest` and `ONrUpdateCheck_Decide`:

| Case | Expect |
| --- | --- |
| Newer tag, published after build time, not skipped | `Available` |
| Same tag as mine (with/without leading `v`) | `None` |
| Latest tag == skipped tag | `None` |
| Latest published_at ≤ my build time (dev build) | `None` |
| my_build_time == 0, newer tag | `Available` (guard disabled) |
| List contains a draft newer than a non-draft | draft excluded; picks newest non-draft |
| Prerelease is newest | picked (v1 includes prereleases) |
| Empty list | `PickLatest` → -1; `Decide(NULL)` → `None` |

## Per-release impact: zero

The release workflow is unchanged — keep cutting `gh release create vX …`. The app reads tags live; no new file, signing key, appcast, or embedded updater. The only soft convention: give each release a tidy `name`, since the prompt surfaces it (falls back to the tag if absent).

## Verification

- **Unit**: the table above, run in the existing C test harness.
- **Manual happy path**: temporarily set the bundle's `CFBundleShortVersionString` to an older value (e.g. `1.3.0r1`), launch, confirm the prompt names the real latest release; click **Download…** → release page opens; relaunch → prompt again (Later) ; click **Skip This Version** → relaunch → no prompt; tick suppression → relaunch → no network call.
- **Offline**: launch in airplane mode → no prompt, launch not delayed beyond the ~2.5 s cap; `lastCheck` not advanced (retries next launch).
- **Current build**: launch the actual latest version → no prompt.

## Forward-compatibility (Sparkle v2, deferred)

The decision core and the "surface a prompt here" seam are reusable. Graduating to Sparkle = embed the framework + publish a signed appcast (the same "latest manifest" idea, plus EdDSA signatures and in-app install). Cheap optional prep, not required by v1: make `CFBundleVersion` a **monotonic integer** (Sparkle compares it). v1's timestamp logic does not need it.

## Risks / what could go sideways

1. **GitHub unauth rate limit (60/hr/IP)** — once-per-launch + 4 h throttle makes this negligible; if hit, the fetch is treated as "no info" and skipped silently.
2. **AppKit readiness when the picker didn't run** — returning users reach the check with `NSApp` not finish-launched. The shell brings AppKit up idempotently (guarding against a double `finishLaunching`); if this proves awkward pre-window, the fallback is to defer the check to just after SDL's Cocoa init and run the `NSAlert` over the window (macOS allows it). Decide during implementation if the idempotent path misbehaves.
3. **`string(TIMESTAMP … %s)` portability** — fine on macOS/Unix; if it ever yields empty, `my_build_time` defaults to 0 and only the dev-build guard is lost.
4. **Repo goes private again** — the unauth API would 404 and the check would silently no-op (no crash). If private distribution is ever wanted, switch the data source to a public manifest (also the natural Sparkle appcast host).
5. **Network stall** — the hard ~2.5 s wait cap and request cancellation bound the launch delay.

## Out of scope (deliberately deferred)

- **Sparkle / true in-app auto-update** — the v2 destination; separate issue when cadence/audience justify the recurring signing+appcast burden.
- **Auto-download / auto-mount the DMG** — reimplements a sliver of Sparkle without its hardening; if we want "no manual drag," do Sparkle properly.
- **In-engine overlay** — hooking Oni's own menu renderer for the prompt. The native dialog is simpler and robust.
- **"Check for Updates…" menu item** — automatic startup check only for v1.
- **Channel picker (Stable/Preview)** — v1 is newest-wins; the `is_prerelease` field is recorded so a future filter is a one-liner.
- **CI-built releases** — releases still cut on the maintainer's Mac.

## File manifest

**New files:**
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/ONi_UpdateCheck.c` (~120 lines) — pure decision core
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/ONi_UpdateCheck.h` (~40 lines)
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Platform_SDL/Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm` (~150 lines) — Cocoa shell
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Platform_SDL/Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h` (~15 lines)
- Unit test file alongside the existing `ONi_GameData` tests

**Modified files:**
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c` (include + one guarded call — ~4 lines)
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniCMakeProjs/OniProj/CMakeLists.txt` (sources + `ONI_BUILD_EPOCH` compile def — ~6 lines)
- `OniARM64/README.md` (one line in the feature list / prose status when it lands, per the workflow contract)
- `OniARM64/HISTORY.md` (session entry per behaviour-changing commit)

**No changes to:** `Info.plist` (we read existing keys), the signing/notarization pipeline, the release workflow, or any other engine source.

## Issue lifecycle

- **[#40](https://github.com/andiyar/OniARM64/issues/40)**: closed by the commit that lands the notifier end-to-end with `fixes #40`, after user verification (prompt appears against a real newer release, buttons behave, offline is silent). Until then, fix attempts use `addresses #40`.
- **Sparkle follow-up**: filed as a new issue only when v2 work begins — not now.

| Network in C vs Obj-C | Fetch + JSON parse + ISO8601→epoch all in the `.mm` (NSURLSession / NSJSONSerialization / NSISO8601DateFormatter). The C core does pure string/int comparison only — no date libs, no I/O |
| State | `NSUserDefaults`: skipped tag, last-check timestamp, opt-out flag |
| Throttle | Skip the network call if checked < ~4 h ago, so relaunches stay instant |
| Launch safety | Hard ~2.5 s cap on the pre-window network wait; offline/slow/rate-limited → silently skip, launch proceeds |
| Opt-out | `NSAlert` suppression checkbox ("Don't check for updates") sets a persistent flag |
| Per-release burden | **Zero.** `gh release create` flow unchanged; no keys, no appcast, no embedded updater |

## Architecture

```
Oni.c (startup, main thread, after game-data resolution, pre-SDL-window)
  └─ ONrUpdateCheck_RunAtStartup()                 [Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm]
        ├─ read NSUserDefaults (disabled? throttled?)         → maybe return early
        ├─ ensure AppKit ready (idempotent; mirrors picker)
        ├─ NSURLSession GET /releases  (UA header, ~2.5s cap) → array of releases
        ├─ parse JSON → ONtReleaseInfo[]  (published_at via NSISO8601DateFormatter)
        ├─ ONrUpdateCheck_PickLatest(...)                     [ONi_UpdateCheck.c, pure]
        ├─ ONrUpdateCheck_Decide(...)                         [ONi_UpdateCheck.c, pure]
        └─ if Available: NSAlert (Download… / Skip / Later + suppression)
              ├─ Download → NSWorkspace openURL: release html_url
              ├─ Skip     → persist skipped tag
              └─ (always)  → persist last-check timestamp
```

Same split the codebase already uses for first-run setup: all the decidable logic is pure, testable C (`ONi_UpdateCheck.c`); the `.mm` is the thin Cocoa shell doing network + dialog. The check runs at the same pre-window, main-thread point as `ONrDataSetup_RunGuidedPicker` ([Oni.c:193](../../../OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c)), so there is no interaction with SDL's event loop or fullscreen state.

## Components

### 1. `ONi_UpdateCheck.c` / `ONi_UpdateCheck.h` — new (pure C, unit-tested)

No Cocoa, no network, no date parsing. Trivially unit-testable.

```c
// ONi_UpdateCheck.h
#define ONcUpdate_TagMax    64
#define ONcUpdate_NameMax   160
#define ONcUpdate_UrlMax    512

typedef enum {
    ONcUpdate_None      = 0,   // up to date, can't tell, or skipped
    ONcUpdate_Available = 1    // a newer, non-skipped release exists
} ONtUpdateVerdict;

typedef struct {
    char     tag[ONcUpdate_TagMax];     // e.g. "v1.3.0r4"
    char     name[ONcUpdate_NameMax];   // display, e.g. "Oni 1.3.0r4 — …" (falls back to tag)
    char     url[ONcUpdate_UrlMax];     // release html_url to open
    long long published_at;             // epoch seconds (filled by the .mm)
    int      is_draft;                  // 1 = draft (always excluded)
    int      is_prerelease;             // recorded; v1 includes prereleases
} ONtReleaseInfo;

// Pick the newest non-draft release. Returns index, or -1 if none.
// Channel policy lives here: v1 ignores is_prerelease (includes all);
// a future "stable only" filter is a one-line change + one test.
int ONrUpdateCheck_PickLatest(const ONtReleaseInfo *releases, int count);

// Pure decision. my_build_time == 0 disables the dev-build guard.
ONtUpdateVerdict ONrUpdateCheck_Decide(
    const char           *my_version,    // CFBundleShortVersionString, e.g. "1.3.0r3"
    long long             my_build_time, // compile-time epoch, 0 = unknown
    const ONtReleaseInfo *latest,        // result of PickLatest, or NULL
    const char           *skipped_tag);  // "" if none
```

**Decision rule** (`ONrUpdateCheck_Decide`), in order:
1. `latest == NULL` → `None`.
2. `latest->tag` equals `skipped_tag` (exact) → `None`.
3. normalise both tags by stripping a single leading `v`; if `latest` normalised == `my_version` → `None` (already on it).
4. `my_build_time != 0` && `latest->published_at <= my_build_time` → `None` (we're newer/equal — local dev-build guard).
5. otherwise → `Available`.

### 2. `Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm` / `Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h` — new (Cocoa shell)

Exposes one C entry point:

```c
// Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h
void ONrUpdateCheck_RunAtStartup(void);   // no-op on non-Apple builds
```

Flow:
1. **Gate**: if `OniUpdateDisabled` is set → return. If `OniUpdateLastCheck` is within the throttle window (~4 h) → return.
2. **AppKit readiness**: if `NSApp` is not yet running (returning users, where the data picker did not run), set `NSApplicationActivationPolicyRegular` + `finishLaunching` + activate — idempotent, mirroring `ONrDataSetup_RunGuidedPicker`. Guard against a double `finishLaunching`.
3. **Fetch**: `NSURLSession` data task to `https://api.github.com/repos/andiyar/OniARM64/releases?per_page=10`, headers `Accept: application/vnd.github+json` and `User-Agent: OniARM64-UpdateCheck` (GitHub requires a UA). Bounded wait ~2.5 s via a dispatch semaphore; on expiry, cancel and return **without** writing `lastCheck` (so a transient offline launch retries next time).
4. **Parse**: `NSJSONSerialization` → fill `ONtReleaseInfo[]`; `published_at` parsed with `NSISO8601DateFormatter` → epoch seconds.
5. **Decide**: `ONrUpdateCheck_PickLatest` then `ONrUpdateCheck_Decide`, passing `my_version` from `[[NSBundle mainBundle] objectForInfoDictionaryKey:@"CFBundleShortVersionString"]` and `my_build_time` from the `ONI_BUILD_EPOCH` compile-time macro.
6. **Prompt** (only if `Available`): `NSAlert`, `messageText` = release name, `informativeText` = "You're running <mine>. Version <new> is now available on GitHub.", buttons **Download…** / **Skip This Version** / **Later**, plus `showsSuppressionButton = YES` ("Don't check for updates"). On Download → `[[NSWorkspace sharedWorkspace] openURL:]` the release `html_url`. On Skip → persist `OniUpdateSkippedTag`. If suppression checked → persist `OniUpdateDisabled`.
7. **Always on a successful fetch**: persist `OniUpdateLastCheck = now`.

`Download` opens the page and returns; the game continues launching (non-disruptive — the user can update after playing).

### 3. `Oni.c` — call site (modified)

- Add `#include "Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h"` near the existing macOS includes (~[Oni.c:66](../../../OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c)).
- Immediately after game-data resolution + its error check (~[Oni.c:202](../../../OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c), before `TMrInitialize`):

```c
#if defined(__APPLE__) && UUmSDL
	ONrUpdateCheck_RunAtStartup();
#endif
```

Data is guaranteed resolved at this point, and we're still pre-window on the main thread.

### 4. `CMakeLists.txt` — modified

In the macOS native Obj-C++ block of [OniProj/OniCMakeProjs/OniProj/CMakeLists.txt:556](../../../OniARM64/OniProj/OniCMakeProjs/OniProj/CMakeLists.txt) (where `Oni_DataSetup_macOS.mm` is added), add:

- Sources: `ONi_UpdateCheck.c`, `ONi_UpdateCheck.h`, `Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm`, `Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h`.
- Compile definition for the build-time guard:

```cmake
string(TIMESTAMP ONI_BUILD_EPOCH "%s" UTC)   # seconds since epoch, at configure time
target_compile_definitions(<target> PRIVATE ONI_BUILD_EPOCH=${ONI_BUILD_EPOCH})
```

`ONi_UpdateCheck.c` compiles on all platforms (pure C); the `.mm` and the `Oni.c` call are already `__APPLE__`/`UUmSDL`-guarded. AppKit/Foundation are already linked for the existing `.mm` units. If `ONI_BUILD_EPOCH` is ever undefined, the code defaults `my_build_time` to `0`, which disables only the dev-build guard (tag-difference still works).

### 5. Unit tests — new

Mirror the existing `ONi_GameData` C-core tests (same harness/location — the plan pins the exact path). Cover `ONrUpdateCheck_PickLatest` and `ONrUpdateCheck_Decide`:

| Case | Expect |
| --- | --- |
| Newer tag, published after build time, not skipped | `Available` |
| Same tag as mine (with/without leading `v`) | `None` |
| Latest tag == skipped tag | `None` |
| Latest published_at ≤ my build time (dev build) | `None` |
| my_build_time == 0, newer tag | `Available` (guard disabled) |
| List contains a draft newer than a non-draft | draft excluded; picks newest non-draft |
| Prerelease is newest | picked (v1 includes prereleases) |
| Empty list | `PickLatest` → -1; `Decide(NULL)` → `None` |

## Per-release impact: zero

The release workflow is unchanged — keep cutting `gh release create vX …`. The app reads tags live; no new file, signing key, appcast, or embedded updater. The only soft convention: give each release a tidy `name`, since the prompt surfaces it (falls back to the tag if absent).

## Verification

- **Unit**: the table above, run in the existing C test harness.
- **Manual happy path**: temporarily set the bundle's `CFBundleShortVersionString` to an older value (e.g. `1.3.0r1`), launch, confirm the prompt names the real latest release; click **Download…** → release page opens; relaunch → prompt again (Later) ; click **Skip This Version** → relaunch → no prompt; tick suppression → relaunch → no network call.
- **Offline**: launch in airplane mode → no prompt, launch not delayed beyond the ~2.5 s cap; `lastCheck` not advanced (retries next launch).
- **Current build**: launch the actual latest version → no prompt.

## Forward-compatibility (Sparkle v2, deferred)

The decision core and the "surface a prompt here" seam are reusable. Graduating to Sparkle = embed the framework + publish a signed appcast (the same "latest manifest" idea, plus EdDSA signatures and in-app install). Cheap optional prep, not required by v1: make `CFBundleVersion` a **monotonic integer** (Sparkle compares it). v1's timestamp logic does not need it.

## Risks / what could go sideways

1. **GitHub unauth rate limit (60/hr/IP)** — once-per-launch + 4 h throttle makes this negligible; if hit, the fetch is treated as "no info" and skipped silently.
2. **AppKit readiness when the picker didn't run** — returning users reach the check with `NSApp` not finish-launched. The shell brings AppKit up idempotently (guarding against a double `finishLaunching`); if this proves awkward pre-window, the fallback is to defer the check to just after SDL's Cocoa init and run the `NSAlert` over the window (macOS allows it). Decide during implementation if the idempotent path misbehaves.
3. **`string(TIMESTAMP … %s)` portability** — fine on macOS/Unix; if it ever yields empty, `my_build_time` defaults to 0 and only the dev-build guard is lost.
4. **Repo goes private again** — the unauth API would 404 and the check would silently no-op (no crash). If private distribution is ever wanted, switch the data source to a public manifest (also the natural Sparkle appcast host).
5. **Network stall** — the hard ~2.5 s wait cap and request cancellation bound the launch delay.

## Out of scope (deliberately deferred)

- **Sparkle / true in-app auto-update** — the v2 destination; separate issue when cadence/audience justify the recurring signing+appcast burden.
- **Auto-download / auto-mount the DMG** — reimplements a sliver of Sparkle without its hardening; if we want "no manual drag," do Sparkle properly.
- **In-engine overlay** — hooking Oni's own menu renderer for the prompt. The native dialog is simpler and robust.
- **"Check for Updates…" menu item** — automatic startup check only for v1.
- **Channel picker (Stable/Preview)** — v1 is newest-wins; the `is_prerelease` field is recorded so a future filter is a one-liner.
- **CI-built releases** — releases still cut on the maintainer's Mac.

## File manifest

**New files:**
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/ONi_UpdateCheck.c` (~120 lines) — pure decision core
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/ONi_UpdateCheck.h` (~40 lines)
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Platform_SDL/Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.mm` (~150 lines) — Cocoa shell
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Platform_SDL/Oni_UpdateCheck_macOS.h` (~15 lines)
- Unit test file alongside the existing `ONi_GameData` tests

**Modified files:**
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniGameSource/Oni.c` (include + one guarded call — ~4 lines)
- `OniARM64/OniProj/OniCMakeProjs/OniProj/CMakeLists.txt` (sources + `ONI_BUILD_EPOCH` compile def — ~6 lines)
- `OniARM64/README.md` (one line in the feature list / prose status when it lands, per the workflow contract)
- `OniARM64/HISTORY.md` (session entry per behaviour-changing commit)

**No changes to:** `Info.plist` (we read existing keys), the signing/notarization pipeline, the release workflow, or any other engine source.

## Issue lifecycle

- **[#40](https://github.com/andiyar/OniARM64/issues/40)**: closed by the commit that lands the notifier end-to-end with `fixes #40`, after user verification (prompt appears against a real newer release, buttons behave, offline is silent). Until then, fix attempts use `addresses #40`.
- **Sparkle follow-up**: filed as a new issue only when v2 work begins — not now.

## computer-use
You have a computer-use MCP available (tools named `mcp__computer-use__*`). It lets you take screenshots of the user's desktop and control it with mouse clicks, keyboard input, and scrolling.

**Pick the right tool for the app.** Each tier trades speed/precision against coverage:

1. **Dedicated MCP for the app** — if the task is in an app that has its own MCP (Slack, Gmail, Calendar, Linear, etc.) and that MCP is connected, use it. API-backed tools are fast and precise.
2. **Chrome MCP** (`mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`) — if the target is a web app and there's no dedicated MCP for it, use the browser tools. DOM-aware, much faster than clicking pixels. If the Chrome extension isn't connected, ask the user to install it rather than falling through to computer use.
3. **Computer use** — for native desktop apps (Maps, Notes, Finder, Photos, System Settings, any third-party native app) and cross-app workflows. Computer use IS the right tool here — don't decline a native-app task just because there's no dedicated MCP for it.

This is about what's available, not error handling — if a dedicated MCP tool errors, debug or report it rather than silently retrying via a slower tier.

**Look before you assert.** If the user asks about app state (what's open, what's connected, what an app can do), take a screenshot and check before answering. Don't answer from memory — the user's setup or app version may differ from what you expect. If you're about to say an app doesn't support an action, that claim should be grounded in what you just saw on screen, not general knowledge. Similarly, `list_granted_applications` or a fresh `screenshot` is cheaper than a wrong assertion about what's running.

**Loading via ToolSearch — load in bulk, not one-by-one:** if computer-use tools are in the deferred list, load them ALL in a single ToolSearch call: `{ query: "computer-use", max_results: 30 }`. The keyword search matches the server-name substring in every tool name, so one query returns the entire toolkit. Don't use `select:` for individual tools — that's one round-trip per tool.

**Access flow:** before any computer-use action you must call `request_access` with the list of applications you need. The user approves each application explicitly, and you may need to call it again mid-task if you discover you need another application.

**Tiered apps:** some apps are granted at a restricted tier based on their category — the tier is displayed in the approval dialog and returned in the `request_access` response:
- **Browsers** (Safari, Chrome, Firefox, Edge, Arc, etc.) → tier **"read"**: visible in screenshots, but clicks and typing are blocked. You can read what's already on screen. For navigation, clicking, or form-filling, use the claude-in-chrome MCP (tools named `mcp__claude-in-chrome__*`; load via ToolSearch if deferred).
- **Terminals and IDEs** (Terminal, iTerm, VS Code, JetBrains, etc.) → tier **"click"**: visible and left-clickable, but typing, key presses, right-click, modifier-clicks, and drag-drop are blocked. You can click a Run button or scroll test output, but cannot type into the editor or integrated terminal, cannot right-click (the context menu has Paste), and cannot drag text onto them. For shell commands, use the Bash tool.
- **Everything else** → tier **"full"**: no restrictions.

The tier is enforced by the frontmost-app check: if a tier-"read" app is in front, `left_click` returns an error; if a tier-"click" app is in front, `type` and `right_click` return errors. The error tells you what tier the app has and what to do instead. `open_application` works at any tier — bringing an app forward is a read-level operation.

**Link safety — treat links in emails and messages as suspicious by default.**
- **Never click web links with computer-use tools.** If you encounter a link in a native app (Mail, Messages, a PDF, etc.), do NOT `left_click` it. Open the URL via the claude-in-chrome MCP instead.
- **See the full URL before following any link.** Visible link text can be misleading — hover or inspect to get the real destination.
- **Links from emails, messages, or unknown-sender documents are suspicious by default.** If the destination URL is at all unfamiliar or looks off, ask the user for confirmation before proceeding.
- **Inside the Chrome extension** you can click links with the extension's tools, but the suspicion check still applies — verify unfamiliar URLs with the user.

**Financial actions - do not execute trades or move money.** Budgeting and accounting apps (Quicken, YNAB, QuickBooks, etc.) are granted at full tier so you can categorize transactions, generate reports, and help the user organize their finances. But never execute a trade, place an order, send money, or initiate a transfer on the user's behalf - always ask the user to perform those actions themselves.
